literal: from √bṛh, 'to grow, to swell, to expand'; the swelling-without-bound, the encompassing reality
invariant_definition: The ultimate referent of Upaniṣadic inquiry and the target of *brahma-jijñāsā*. In Vedānta the term does not merely mean a highest being; it names that from which the world arises, by which it is sustained, into which it resolves, and on knowing which the bondage of *saṃsāra* is ended. The term therefore sits inside a technical matrix: *jagat-kāraṇatva*, debates over *upādāna* and *nimitta-kāraṇa*, the *sat-kārya* question of whether effects pre-exist in their cause, the opposition of *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa*, and the relation between *brahman*, *īśvara*, *paramātman*, and *bhagavān*. All Vedāntic schools agree that *brahman* is the purport of the Upaniṣads and the reality marked by *janmādy asya yataḥ*; they disagree on whether the world is a real mode, a real power, or an apparent manifestation of that reality, and on whether differentiation belongs to *brahman* itself or only to what depends on it. In practice the term also governs *upāsanā*: the realized object of liberating knowledge for Advaita, and the real Lord to be known, served, and loved for the theistic schools.

per_school.definition: Brahman is articulated as genuinely two-formed, *mūrta* and *amūrta*, and the subsequent *neti neti* functions as a delimitative excess over predication rather than as a retraction of that articulation [[BṛU 2.3.1, 2.3.6](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3)]. The *mahāvākya* *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* is therefore preserved as a real identity-statement, not reduced to a merely figurative *upāsanā-vākya* [[BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)].

Brahman is *ātmaprakāśa* and *ānanda-svabhāva*, with finite pleasures registering only as delimited measures of that plenitude [[BS 1.1 (opening ānanda discussion)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-discussion-of-nanda)]. The apprehension of plurality is *māyā-nibandhana*, and *avidyā* is neither *brahma-svabhāva* nor a sheer non-entity but an *anirvacanīya mithyāvabhāsa* whose locus is the *jīvas* [[BS 1.1 (plurality as māyā-bound)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-ekatva-and-m-y); [BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. Accordingly *kāraṇa-brahman* is established as *anirvacanīya-māyā-viśiṣṭa*, and the *mahāvākya* yields *aparokṣa* only through the aid of *manana* and allied disciplines, so that *brahma-vidyā* is the *nirvikalpaka mano-vṛtti* whose object is the *advitīyātman* [[PV p. 212](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇo lakṣaṇopalakṣaṇe); [PV p. 103](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam); [BR 1.1 ṭīkā](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1)].
primary_loci: Brahma-Siddhi 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

citations.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

per_school.definition: By the word *brahman* one intends *Puruṣottama*, intrinsically free of every *doṣa* and possessed of *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*, so that the *brahma-śabda* takes its primary force in the *sarveśvara* himself [[Śrī-Bhāṣya, Commentary 6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6); [Śrī-Bhāṣya, Commentary 11](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/11)]. This *brahman* is Nārāyaṇa as *antaryāmin*, the single cause of the *cit-acit* cosmos and the inner self of all other entities, which stand to him through *śakti*-, *aṃśa*-, *vibhūti*-, *rūpa*-, and *śarīra*-vācaka expressions in *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

Brahman is Viṣṇu, *nirdoṣa*, *nityapūrṇa* in all *guṇas*, and the unique *svatantra* under whose *vaśa* all other beings stand [[VTV 3.1](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1); [VTV 3.2](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.2)]. For the *mumukṣu* *brahman* alone is the proper *jñeya*, and *ānanda*-language is read through an explicit hierarchy in which finite enjoyers are delimited while the *ānandamaya* bears fullness without remainder [[TD 1.4](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4); [TD 1.1](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1)].

The *abheda-śāstras* concerning *brahman* are retained at the level of shared *cid-rūpatva*, while the *jīva-samūha* stands to Bhagavān through his *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* as ray-atoms stand to the sun, so that *vyatireka* and *avyatireka* are jointly upheld [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once the distinction between the *māyā-āśraya* and the *māyā-mohita* is fixed, *bhajana* of him alone becomes *abhidheya* and Bhagavān is established as the *parama-svarūpa* and proper *viṣaya* of *parama-prema* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44); [Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

*Parabrahman* is Kṛṣṇa/Hari, *svabhāvataḥ apāsta-samasta-doṣa* and the *eka-rāśi* of *aśeṣa-kalyāṇa-guṇas* [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)]. *Tattva* is triadic as *cit*, *acit*, and *brahman*, with *brahman* and *jīva* standing as *svādhīna* and *parādhīna* [[Vedānta-Kaustubha, BS 1.1.1](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1)]. The *jīva* is neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna*, but *brahman*'s *aṃśa* understood as *śakti* and hence real non-separation with real distinction [[Vedānta-Kaustubha, BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42); [VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)].

Brahman itself is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa*, pervading its effects as *sat-cit-ānanda* while *nāma* and *rūpa* belong to the *kārya*-side [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Its manifestation is a real *pariṇāma* in *kāryākāra*, yet *avikṛta* in the manner of gold becoming ornaments, so that *brahman*'s plenitude is not diminished by emergence [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. The *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ*, and *brahmajñāna* matures into *Puruṣottama-prāpti* only through the *bhakti-bhāva* that renders the soul fit for divine appropriation [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45); [BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

In the Bhikṣu synthesis, *brahman* is not approached through an epistemic thinning of *māyā* into mere *avidyā*; in *Śvetāśvatara* 4.10 *māyā* is read as *prakṛti* itself, preserving a real causal apparatus for manifestation [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. The *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ* in an expressive and dependent sense, like a son to a father, and yogic *īśvara-praṇidhāna* is accordingly a *samādhi-bhāvanā* oriented to a *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by *kleśa-karma-vipākāśaya* [[VĀB 2.3.43](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43); [YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23); [YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)].

translator_note: Avoid lazy equivalences such as 'the Absolute' or 'God' without qualification. 'The Absolute' imports German-idealist baggage; 'God' imports a personal creator model that fits some Vedāntic schools and not others. The Sanskrit term carries a causal and soteriological apparatus: world-ground, object of liberating knowledge, and, in many schools, the real Lord of worship. Also keep the distinctions clear: *brahman* (neuter) is not *brahmā* (the creator deity) and not *brāhmaṇa* (the ritual-social designation).
